docs.title: OracleTrace: Python Performance Regression Detection
docs.text:
OracleTrace helps you find Python slowdowns early by tracing function calls, comparing execution runs, and showing a clean call graph.

Get started in 2 minutes Install OracleTrace

docs.title: What is OracleTrace?
docs.text:
OracleTrace is a lightweight Python execution tracing tool built for practical performance analysis.

Use it to:

  • detect performance regressions between script versions
  • compare baseline and new trace runs
  • inspect per-function timing and call counts
  • visualize caller to callee flow with a readable tree
  • automate performance checks in CI pipelines

docs.title: Why use it instead of a heavy profiler?
docs.text:
Most profilers are great for deep optimization work. OracleTrace is focused on quick day-to-day regression checks.

You get fast answers to questions like:

  • What became slower after this change?
  • Which function improved?
  • Did new calls appear in this run?

docs.title: Performance comparison
docs.text:
Compare two JSON traces and instantly spot:

  • Slower functions
  • Faster functions
  • New functions
  • Removed functions

docs.title: Execution trace metrics
docs.text:
For each function:

  • Total execution time
  • Call count
  • Average time per call
  • Caller and callee relationships

docs.title: Call graph visualization
docs.text: Understand execution flow at a glance through a structured logic tree.

docs.title: JSON export for automation
docs.text:
Store results and plug them into:

  • CI performance gates
  • historical regression tracking
  • custom analysis scripts

docs.title: Quick command preview
docs.text:
oracletrace my_app.py
oracletrace my_app.py --json baseline.json
oracletrace my_app.py --json new.json --compare baseline.json

docs.title: How OracleTrace works
docs.text:
OracleTrace uses Python's built-in sys.setprofile() hook to capture call and return events, then aggregates function-level timing and call relationships.

It filters out non-project code so output stays focused on what you actually own.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Installation guide
  • Quickstart tutorial
  • CLI reference
  • Practical examples

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Complete command reference for OracleTrace.

docs.title: Command syntax
docs.text: oracletrace <target> [--json OUTPUT.json] [--compare BASELINE.json]

docs.title: target
docs.text:
Path to the Python script you want to trace.

Example:

oracletrace my_app.py

docs.title: --json
docs.text:
Exports trace results to a JSON file.

oracletrace my_app.py --json run.json

Use this when you want to keep historical traces or compare later.

docs.title: --compare
docs.text:
Compares the current run against a previous JSON trace.

oracletrace my_app.py --json current.json --compare baseline.json

Comparison output includes:

  • Functions with performance increase or decrease
  • Newly added functions
  • Removed functions

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
OracleTrace returns a non-zero exit code when:

  • The target script does not exist
  • The compare JSON file does not exist

Non-zero exit codes can also happen when the target script fails at runtime or when the compare JSON file cannot be parsed.

docs.title: Local development workflow
docs.text:
oracletrace app.py --json baseline.json
# change code
oracletrace app.py --json current.json --compare baseline.json

docs.title: CI regression workflow
docs.text:
oracletrace app.py --json current.json --compare baseline.json

Store baseline.json as a known-good artifact.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Run from your project root so filtering focuses on your app code
  • Keep baseline and current runs as similar as possible
  • Use deterministic input data for reliable comparisons

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Practical OracleTrace usage patterns for local development and CI.

docs.title: Basic trace run
docs.text:
oracletrace my_script.py

Best for:

  • Quickly identifying heavy functions
  • Understanding call flow before optimization

docs.title: Save trace data to JSON
docs.text:
oracletrace my_script.py --json trace.json

Best for:

  • Keeping historical performance snapshots
  • Sharing results between local and CI environments

docs.title: Compare two executions
docs.text:
oracletrace my_script.py --json new.json --compare baseline.json

This highlights function-level deltas so you can catch regressions right after a change.

docs.title: Compare two code versions
docs.text:
# on version A
oracletrace app.py --json v1.json

# on version B
oracletrace app.py --json v2.json --compare v1.json

Great for release validation and refactor checks.

docs.title: Lightweight CI check pattern
docs.text:
# one-time baseline (store artifact)
oracletrace my_script.py --json baseline.json

# in CI pipeline
oracletrace my_script.py --json current.json --compare baseline.json

Use this when you want a simple, scriptable guardrail before merging changes.

docs.title: Read the logic flow tree
docs.text:
Typical output shape:

<module>
└── app.py:main
    ├── app.py:load_data
    └── app.py:process_data

This is useful for spotting unexpected call paths after feature updates.

docs.title: Tips for accurate comparisons
docs.text:
• Keep input data consistent between runs
  • Capture a stable baseline from a clean environment
  • Compare after focused changes (small PRs are easier to diagnose)
  • Combine with unit tests for correctness plus performance confidence

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Install OracleTrace in under a minute and get ready to trace Python performance regressions.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • pip

docs.title: Standard install
docs.text: pip install oracletrace

docs.title: Verify install
docs.text:
oracletrace --help

You should see command usage, required target script, and available options.

docs.title: Recommended: use a virtual environment
docs.text:
python -m venv .venv

Activate it:

Linux/macOSWindows (PowerShell) 
source .venv/bin/activate

.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

Then install:

pip install oracletrace

docs.title: Upgrade to latest version
docs.text: pip install --upgrade oracletrace

docs.title: Dependency note
docs.text: OracleTrace uses rich for readable terminal output and installs it automatically.

docs.title: oracletrace command not found
docs.text:
Try running with Python module mode:

python -m oracletrace --help

If that works, your scripts directory is probably missing from PATH.

docs.title: Python version error
docs.text:
Check your Python version:

python --version

OracleTrace requires Python 3.10 or newer.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This quickstart shows how to trace a script, create a baseline, and detect a performance regression.

docs.title: 1. Create a sample script
docs.text:
Create a file named my_app.py:

import time


def process_data():
    time.sleep(0.10)
    calculate_results()


def calculate_results():
    time.sleep(0.20)


def main():
    for _ in range(2):
        process_data()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

docs.title: 2. Run a trace
docs.text:
oracletrace my_app.py

You will see:

  • Top functions by total time
  • Call counts and average time per call
  • Call graph logic flow

docs.title: 3. Save a baseline trace
docs.text:
oracletrace my_app.py --json baseline.json

Keep this file as your reference run.

docs.title: 4. Simulate a regression and compare
docs.text:
Increase one of the sleep values in your script, then run:

oracletrace my_app.py --json new.json --compare baseline.json

Comparison output highlights:

  • Functions that got slower
  • Functions that got faster
  • Newly added or removed functions

docs.title: 5. Understand the output quickly
docs.text:
Example flow:

<module>
└── my_app.py:main
    └── my_app.py:process_data
        └── my_app.py:calculate_results

OracleTrace is optimized for relative change detection between runs, which makes it ideal for everyday checks and CI validation.
